Use monthly variables for model features.

title: Prototype: Clustering Analysis
text:
This prototype now follows a tighter analytical rhythm: one focused code block, one direct output, and one explanation. The goal is to keep the page readable and fully reproducible at the same time.
The research question is:

Can monthly tourism observations be grouped into a few interpretable market states defined by visitor arrivals, China’s market share, hotel occupancy, and length of stay?

The method remains k-means clustering on standardized monthly indicators, because this dataset is continuous and month-based rather than respondent-based.

This prototype now follows a tighter analytical rhythm: one focused code block, one direct output, and one explanation. The goal is to keep the page readable and fully reproducible at the same time.
The research question is:

Can monthly tourism observations be grouped into a few interpretable market states defined by visitor arrivals, China’s market share, hotel occupancy, and length of stay?

The method remains k-means clustering on standardized monthly indicators, because this dataset is continuous and month-based rather than respondent-based.

title: Prototype: Clustering Analysis
text:
7. Standardize The Modeling Variables
K-means depends on Euclidean distance, so the four variables must be standardized before clustering. Otherwise, visitor counts would dominate the distance calculation simply because they are measured on a much larger scale.

cluster_matrix <- scale(as.matrix(cluster_input[, feature_vars, drop = FALSE]))

standardization_check <- data.frame(
  Variable = colnames(cluster_matrix),
  Mean_after_scaling = round(colMeans(cluster_matrix), 3),
  SD_after_scaling = round(apply(cluster_matrix, 2, sd), 3)
)

title: Prototype: Clustering Analysis
text:
8. Generate The Elbow Plot
The first diagnostic for cluster selection is the elbow method. It compares how the within-cluster sum of squares changes as k increases.

candidate_k <- 2:6
distance_matrix <- dist(cluster_matrix)

model_metrics <- bind_rows(lapply(candidate_k, function(k) {
  set.seed(42)
  fit <- kmeans(cluster_matrix, centers = k, nstart = 25)
  sil <- silhouette(fit$cluster, distance_matrix)

  data.frame(
    k = k,
    tot_withinss = fit$tot.withinss,
    silhouette = mean(sil[, "sil_width"], na.rm = TRUE)
  )
}))

ggplot(model_metrics, aes(x = k, y = tot_withinss)) +
  geom_line(color = "#0f3d3e", linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(color = "#d86f45", size = 2.6) +
  scale_x_continuous(breaks = candidate_k) +
  labs(
    title = "Elbow Plot for Candidate Cluster Counts",
    x = "Number of clusters (k)",
    y = "Total within-cluster sum of squares"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)







Figure 1





The elbow bends clearly by around k = 3, which suggests that moving beyond three clusters yields diminishing returns in compactness.

title: Prototype: Clustering Analysis
text:
9. Generate The Silhouette Plot
The second diagnostic is the mean silhouette score, which measures how well-separated the clusters are.

ggplot(model_metrics, aes(x = factor(k), y = silhouette, fill = k == 3)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.68, show.legend = FALSE) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("TRUE" = "#0f6b5b", "FALSE" = "#c7d4cf")) +
  labs(
    title = "Mean Silhouette Score by Candidate k",
    x = "Number of clusters (k)",
    y = "Mean silhouette score"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)







Figure 2





The silhouette score is highest at k = r model_metrics$k[which.max(model_metrics$silhouette)], but k = 3 remains a defensible choice because it creates a more meaningful market-state interpretation than a simple two-cluster split.

title: Prototype: Clustering Analysis
text:
10. Compare The Cluster Selection Metrics
The two diagnostics can be read together in tabular form before fixing the final model.

metrics_table <- model_metrics |>
  transmute(
    K = k,
    `Total within-cluster SS` = round(tot_withinss, 1),
    `Mean silhouette` = round(silhouette, 3)
  )

knitr::kable(metrics_table, align = c("r", "r", "r"))

title: Prototype: Clustering Analysis
text:
11. Fit The Final K-Means Model
Once k = 3 is selected, the final model can be estimated and the month-level cluster assignments can be generated.

selected_k <- 3
set.seed(42)
cluster_fit <- kmeans(cluster_matrix, centers = selected_k, nstart = 25)
pca_fit <- prcomp(cluster_matrix)

cluster_results <- cluster_input |>
  mutate(
    cluster_id = cluster_fit$cluster,
    pc1 = pca_fit$x[, 1],
    pc2 = pca_fit$x[, 2]
  )

cluster_profile_raw <- cluster_results |>
  group_by(cluster_id) |>
  summarise(
    months = n(),
    visitor_arrivals = mean(visitor_arrivals),
    china_share = mean(china_share),
    hotel_occ = mean(hotel_occ),
    avg_stay_monthly = mean(avg_stay_monthly),
    avg_stay_monthly_capped = mean(avg_stay_monthly_capped),
    .groups = "drop"
  )

label_map <- cluster_profile_raw |>
  arrange(visitor_arrivals) |>
  mutate(
    state_label = c(
      "Pandemic shock months",
      "Recovery transition months",
      "High-performance months"
    )
  ) |>
  select(cluster_id, state_label)

cluster_results <- cluster_results |>
  left_join(label_map, by = "cluster_id") |>
  mutate(
    state_label = factor(
      state_label,
      levels = c(
        "Pandemic shock months",
        "Recovery transition months",
        "High-performance months"
      )
    ),
    period = factor(period, levels = c("pre_covid", "covid_shock", "recovery"))
  )

cluster_size_table <- cluster_results |>
  count(state_label, name = "Months") |>
  arrange(state_label)

largest_state_name <- as.character(cluster_size_table$state_label[which.max(cluster_size_table$Months)])
largest_state_months <- cluster_size_table$Months[which.max(cluster_size_table$Months)]

knitr::kable(cluster_size_table, align = c("l", "r"))

title: Prototype: Clustering Analysis
text:
13. Plot The Cluster Separation
The next figure projects the standardized monthly observations onto two principal components so that the three cluster states can be viewed in a reduced feature space.

state_palette <- c(
  "Pandemic shock months" = "#b6405d",
  "Recovery transition months" = "#ef9f23",
  "High-performance months" = "#0f6b5b"
)

ggplot(cluster_results, aes(x = pc1, y = pc2, color = state_label, shape = period)) +
  geom_point(size = 3.1, alpha = 0.88) +
  scale_color_manual(values = state_palette) +
  labs(
    title = "Cluster State Map in Reduced Feature Space",
    x = "Principal component 1",
    y = "Principal component 2",
    color = "Market state",
    shape = "Period"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)







Figure 3





The scatter plot shows that the market states are not randomly mixed. The strongest separation appears between the pandemic shock months and the high-performance months, while the recovery transition months sit between them.

title: Prototype: Clustering Analysis
text:
14. Compare The State Profiles
To make the clusters easier to interpret, the next table summarizes the average market conditions inside each inferred state.

cluster_profile <- cluster_profile_raw |>
  left_join(label_map, by = "cluster_id") |>
  mutate(
    state_label = factor(
      state_label,
      levels = c(
        "Pandemic shock months",
        "Recovery transition months",
        "High-performance months"
      )
    )
  ) |>
  arrange(state_label)

profile_table <- cluster_profile |>
  transmute(
    `Market state` = state_label,
    Months = months,
    `Mean visitor arrivals` = format(round(visitor_arrivals), big.mark = ","),
    `Mean China share` = sprintf("%.1f%%", 100 * china_share),
    `Mean hotel occupancy` = sprintf("%.1f%%", hotel_occ),
    `Mean stay (raw)` = round(avg_stay_monthly, 1),
    `Mean stay (capped for model)` = round(avg_stay_monthly_capped, 1)
  )

title: Prototype: Clustering Analysis
text:
15. Plot The State Timeline
The clustering result also needs to be anchored back to historical time.

ggplot(cluster_results, aes(x = date, y = state_label, color = state_label)) +
  geom_point(size = 2.8, alpha = 0.9) +
  scale_color_manual(values = state_palette) +
  labs(
    title = "Timeline of Inferred Market States",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Market state",
    color = "Market state"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12)







Figure 4





The timeline shows that the clustering result is historically coherent: pandemic shock months cluster inside the disruption period, recovery transition months bridge the early rebound, and high-performance months dominate the pre-COVID and later recovery periods.

title: Prototype: Clustering Analysis
text:
16. Compare States Across Periods
The final historical check is to cross-tabulate the inferred states against the predefined project periods.

period_state_view <- as.data.frame.matrix(with(cluster_results, table(state_label, period)))
period_state_view$state_label <- rownames(period_state_view)
rownames(period_state_view) <- NULL

period_state_view <- period_state_view |>
  select(state_label, pre_covid, covid_shock, recovery) |>
  transmute(
    `Market state` = state_label,
    `Pre-COVID` = pre_covid,
    `COVID shock` = covid_shock,
    Recovery = recovery
  )

high_perf_pre_covid <- period_state_view$`Pre-COVID`[period_state_view$`Market state` == "High-performance months"]
high_perf_recovery <- period_state_view$Recovery[period_state_view$`Market state` == "High-performance months"]
shock_covid <- period_state_view$`COVID shock`[period_state_view$`Market state` == "Pandemic shock months"]

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics CDA
text: The exploratory data analysis (EDA) identified several key patterns in Singapore’s tourism data, including the sharp decline in visitor arrivals during the COVID-19 period and potential relationships between tourism indicators. Based on these observations, confirmatory data analysis (CDA) is conducted to statistically test these relationships.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics CDA
text:
CDA (Confirmatory Data Analysis)

CDA 1 - Statistical Impact of COVID-19 on Visitor Arrivals
Did the COVID-19 pandemic significantly affect visitor arrivals to Singapore?
Hypothesis
H₀ (Null Hypothesis) There is no significant difference in visitor arrivals across the tourism periods.
H₁ (Alternative Hypothesis) Visitor arrivals differ significantly across the tourism periods.

plot_ly(
  data = data_clean,
  x = ~period,
  y = ~visitor_arrivals,
  color = ~period,
  type = "box",
  boxpoints = "outliers"
) %>%
  layout(
    title = "Visitor Arrivals Across Tourism Periods",
    xaxis = list(title = "Period"),
    yaxis = list(title = "Visitor Arrivals (Millions)"),
    showlegend = FALSE
  )






anova_model <- aov(visitor_arrivals ~ period, data = data_clean)
summary(anova_model)

             Df    Sum Sq   Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)    
period        2 3.133e+13 1.566e+13   186.9 <2e-16 ***
Residuals   105 8.801e+12 8.382e+10                   
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1








Note



The boxplot compares the distribution of visitor arrivals across different tourism periods. A substantial decline in the median visitor arrivals is observed during the COVID-19 period compared to the pre-COVID period, indicating a significant disruption to tourism demand. The interquartile range also shifts downward during the pandemic, suggesting that the overall level of visitor arrivals was consistently lower across most observations. In contrast, the recovery period shows an upward shift in the distribution, reflecting the gradual rebound of tourism activity after travel restrictions were lifted. Overall, the distributional differences across periods support the conclusion that the COVID-19 pandemic had a significant impact on Singapore’s tourism sector.




CDA 2 - Influence of Chinese Visitor Share on Total Visitor Arrivals
Does the share of Chinese visitors significantly influence total visitor arrivals to Singapore?
Hypothesis
H₀ (Null Hypothesis) China visitor share has no significant relationship with total visitor arrivals.
H₁ (Alternative Hypothesis) China visitor share significantly influences total visitor arrivals.

ggplot(data_clean, aes(x = china_share, y = visitor_arrivals)) +
  geom_point(alpha = 0.6) +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", color = "red") +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship Between China Visitor Share and Total Visitor Arrivals",
    x = "China Visitor Share",
    y = "Visitor Arrivals"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









model_cda2 <- lm(visitor_arrivals ~ china_share, data = data_clean)
summary(model_cda2)


Call:
lm(formula = visitor_arrivals ~ china_share, data = data_clean)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-1133928  -612539   279205   479648   798720 

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)  1151022     103205   11.15   <2e-16 ***
china_share  -680992     486276   -1.40    0.164    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 609700 on 106 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.01817,   Adjusted R-squared:  0.008903 
F-statistic: 1.961 on 1 and 106 DF,  p-value: 0.1643








Tip



p-value > 0.05, H₀ (Null Hypothesis) Cannot be rejected


A linear regression analysis was conducted to examine the relationship between China’s visitor share and total visitor arrivals. The results indicate that the relationship is not statistically significant, as the coefficient for China’s visitor share is negative but insignificant (β = -680,992, p = 0.164). Since the p-value is greater than the 0.05 significance level, the null hypothesis cannot be rejected. This suggests that variations in China’s visitor share do not have a statistically significant impact on total visitor arrivals. Additionally, the low R-squared value ( \(R^2\) = 0.018) indicates that China’s visitor share explains only a small proportion of the variation in overall tourism demand.


CDA 3 - Seasonal Variation in Visitor Arrivals
Do visitor arrivals vary significantly across different months?
Hypothesis
H₀ (Null Hypothesis) Visitor arrivals do not differ significantly across months.
H₁ (Alternative Hypothesis) Visitor arrivals vary significantly across months, indicating seasonal tourism patterns.

data_clean <- data_clean %>%
  mutate(month = month(date, label = TRUE, abbr = TRUE))
data_no_covid <- data_clean %>%
  filter(!(year %in% c(2020, 2021)))


p <- ggplot(
  data_no_covid,
  aes(
    x = month,
    y = visitor_arrivals,
    text = paste(
      "Month:", month,
      "<br>Visitor Arrivals:", format(visitor_arrivals, big.mark = ",")
    )
  )
) +
  geom_boxplot(fill = "skyblue") +
  labs(
    title = "Seasonal Variation in Visitor Arrivals",
    x = "Month",
    y = "Visitor Arrivals"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p, tooltip = "text")






anova_month <- aov(visitor_arrivals ~ month, data = data_no_covid)

summary(anova_month)

            Df    Sum Sq   Mean Sq F value Pr(>F)
month       11 8.784e+11 7.986e+10   0.525  0.881
Residuals   72 1.096e+13 1.522e+11               








Tip




The monthly distributions are based on observations from 2017–2025, with the COVID shock years (2020–2021) excluded to avoid distortion in seasonal patterns.
Pr(>F): Probability of observing an F value greater than the calculated F-statistic under the null hypothesis. (It is the p-value associated with the F-statistic.)



A one-way ANOVA test was conducted to examine whether visitor arrivals differ significantly across months. The analysis was performed using observations from 2017–2025, excluding the COVID shock years (2020–2021) to avoid distortion in seasonal patterns. The boxplot illustrates the distribution of visitor arrivals for each month, allowing potential seasonal variation to be visually assessed. However, the ANOVA results indicate that the effect of month on visitor arrivals is not statistically significant (F = 0.525, p = 0.881). Since the p-value is greater than the significance level of 0.05, the null hypothesis cannot be rejected. This suggests that visitor arrivals do not vary significantly across months in the dataset, indicating limited evidence of strong seasonal patterns in tourism demand during the analyzed period.

All analysis code should use repository-relative paths instead of machine-specific local paths.
Team members should run quarto render before submitting merge-ready changes.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
Singapore’s tourism recovery should not be read as a simple question of whether total arrivals have returned to their pre-pandemic level. The monthly tourism record shows a more layered story: visitor arrivals collapsed during the COVID shock, hotel occupancy recovered at a different pace, the Chinese source market did not rebound in exactly the same pattern as total arrivals, and some months behave like distinct market states rather than points on one smooth trajectory.
This proposal draws directly on the team’s completed prototype work in EDA, CDA, and Clustering. Together, these modules show that Singapore’s recovery is best understood as a monthly market-condition problem rather than a traveller-profile problem. The proposed visual analytics application will therefore focus on state changes over time, cross-variable relationships, and interpretable analytical modules that help users explain both disruption and recovery.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
2. Problem Statement
Public discussion of tourism recovery often reduces the issue to a single volume indicator: whether arrivals are up or down. That framing is too narrow for this project. For Singapore, recovery also involves:

shifts in the contribution of the Chinese source market,
differences between tourism demand and hotel utilisation,
possible structural breaks between pre-COVID, shock, and recovery periods, and
the emergence of different tourism market states across months.

Without an integrated visual analytics workflow, these relationships remain fragmented across disconnected charts and summary tables. The project therefore needs a coordinated prototype that combines descriptive views, confirmatory analysis, clustering, and predictive modeling in one coherent interface.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
4. Data and Preparation
The project uses a cleaned monthly tourism workbook sourced from CEIC. The main analytical sheet spans December 2016 to January 2026 and treats each month as one observation.

Core monthly variables

date
visitor_arrivals
visitor_arrivals_china
hotel_occ
avg_stay_monthly



Derived monthly variables

china_share
year
month
quarter
period
avg_stay_monthly_capped



Supplementary contextual variables

spend_per_capita
tourism_receipts
avg_stay_annual

The analytical workflow prioritizes monthly variables because the main research questions focus on tourism market conditions over time. Annual variables are retained as supporting context but are not treated as the core inputs for clustering or occupancy classification.
The project also adopts a period-based interpretation of the timeline:

pre_covid: December 2016 to January 2020
covid_shock: February 2020 to December 2021
recovery: January 2022 to January 2026

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
5. Research Questions
The proposal is guided by four project-level questions:

How did Singapore’s tourism market differ across the pre-COVID, COVID shock, and recovery periods?
Did the Chinese source market recover in step with the broader inbound tourism market?
Can monthly observations be grouped into a small number of interpretable tourism market states?
Which indicators are most useful for explaining low, medium, and high hotel occupancy outcomes?

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
6. Analytical Approach
The current proposal is no longer a purely conceptual plan. It is informed by working prototypes that already define the analytical logic of the application.

6.1 Exploratory Data Analysis
The exploratory data analysis module provides the descriptive entry point of the proposed visual analytics system. Rather than starting with a predictive model, the project first establishes how the tourism market moved over time and how different indicators changed together. This module is built from interactive time-series charts and relationship plots that help users read the recovery as a sequence of changing monthly conditions instead of a single headline number.
The completed EDA prototype addresses four descriptive questions. First, it shows how total visitor arrivals changed across the pre-COVID, COVID shock, and recovery periods. Second, it compares total arrivals with Chinese arrivals and China’s market share. Third, it examines how hotel occupancy responded to changes in tourism demand. Fourth, it checks whether spending behavior moved in the same direction as visitor volume. These views are especially useful at the proposal stage because they define the variables, visual forms, and interactions that the final application should preserve.
One of the clearest findings from the EDA work is that Singapore’s recovery cannot be understood through total arrivals alone. The China-focused comparative view shows that Chinese arrivals dropped with the broader market during the disruption period, but their rebound after 2022 lagged behind the recovery in total arrivals. This means that Singapore’s tourism recovery was supported by a more diversified mix of source markets rather than a simple restoration of the old demand structure.



EDA: China Market Contribution and Visitor Share


The EDA prototype also demonstrates that hotel occupancy remains strongly connected to visitor arrivals, but not in a perfectly mechanical way. During the COVID shock period, arrivals collapsed to near zero while hotel occupancy remained above zero, which implies that domestic demand, essential travel, and other non-tourism hotel uses still mattered. In the recovery period, occupancy rose alongside arrivals, making this relationship an important bridge between descriptive analysis and the later occupancy-classification module.



EDA: Tourism Demand and Hotel Utilisation


From a proposal perspective, this module contributes two design decisions to the final application. First, the app should foreground linked time-series views that let users compare market volume and source-market composition at the same time. Second, the app should retain relationship plots that connect demand indicators to hotel performance, because these visuals help users see why the same month can represent very different tourism conditions even when total arrivals look similar.


6.2 Confirmatory Data Analysis
The confirmatory data analysis module extends the EDA findings by asking whether the strongest visual patterns remain meaningful once they are tested more formally. In the current prototype, this section is organized around three questions: whether visitor arrivals differ across historical periods, whether China’s visitor share is associated with total arrivals, and whether there is strong month-level seasonality after the most distorted shock years are handled separately.
The current CDA workflow uses a mix of boxplots, ANOVA, regression, and monthly comparison views. This is important for the project because the final Shiny application should not only show users attractive charts; it should also help them distinguish between patterns that are visually striking and patterns that are analytically defensible. In other words, CDA gives the project its evaluation layer.
The prototype results already suggest that period is the most reliable explanatory structure in the dataset. Visitor arrivals clearly differ across the pre-COVID, shock, and recovery periods, which supports the use of period-based filters and period-aware storytelling in the final application. By contrast, the China-share regression view is more cautious: it shows that a higher China share does not automatically translate into higher total arrivals, which means the Chinese market is important but should not be treated as a single dominant driver of overall recovery.



CDA: China Share and Total Visitor Arrivals


This module therefore contributes an important logical safeguard to the proposal. It prevents the final application from making overly simple claims such as “China recovered, therefore tourism recovered” or “seasonality explains the recovery pattern.” Instead, the application can use CDA results to frame more disciplined interpretations, where period effects are emphasized, source-market composition is treated carefully, and statistical summaries sit beside visual exploration rather than being replaced by it.


6.3 Clustering Analysis
The clustering module, developed from Wang Zhuoran’s prototype work, adds a structural layer that neither EDA nor CDA can provide on their own. Instead of asking only whether one variable increased or decreased, it asks whether the monthly observations naturally form a small number of interpretable tourism market states. This is central to the project logic because the unit of analysis is the month, and the core analytical question is whether recovery unfolded through distinct market conditions rather than one smooth trend.
The model uses four standardized monthly indicators: visitor_arrivals, china_share, hotel_occ, and avg_stay_monthly_capped. These variables were chosen because together they capture tourism scale, market composition, accommodation performance, and stay behavior. Standardization is necessary so that visitor counts do not dominate the clustering simply because they are measured on a larger numeric scale than the other indicators.
The prototype first evaluates candidate cluster counts using elbow and silhouette diagnostics. This step matters because the goal is not to force an arbitrary number of groups onto the data, but to justify a cluster structure that is both statistically reasonable and substantively interpretable. The diagnostics support selecting k = 3, which offers a workable balance between compactness and interpretability.



Clustering: Elbow Diagnostic for Selecting k


After fitting the final k-means model, the three cluster states can be interpreted as pandemic shock months, recovery transition months, and high-performance months. The reduced-space cluster map shows that these states are not random labels. They occupy different regions of the feature space and align with meaningful differences in arrivals, hotel occupancy, and China’s market contribution.



Clustering: State Map in Reduced Feature Space


The historical timeline view makes the interpretation even more persuasive. Pandemic shock months concentrate inside the disruption period, recovery transition months bridge the early reopening phase, and high-performance months dominate the pre-COVID and later recovery periods. This is exactly the kind of structure the final application should expose to users: not only whether recovery happened, but what kind of market state each month represents.



Clustering: Timeline of Inferred Market States


In the final Shiny application, this module will support exploratory state comparison, timeline interpretation, and state-level downloads. It is also an effective bridge between descriptive analysis and model-based explanation, because the inferred states provide a compact summary of how multiple tourism indicators move together over time.


6.4 Decision Tree and Random Forest
The decision tree and random forest module focuses on hotel occupancy classification. The goal is to identify which tourism indicators best explain whether a month belongs to a low, medium, or high occupancy state.
The decision tree component emphasizes interpretability by expressing model logic as explicit branching rules. The random forest component strengthens the analysis by testing the same question with a more robust ensemble method, allowing the team to compare predictive stability and variable importance. Together, these methods support both explanation and validation.



Decision Tree Visualization





Random Forest Confusion Matrix





Random Forest Variable Importance

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
7. Proposed Visual and Interaction Design
The final prototype is designed as a modular visual analytics system rather than a single static report. The interaction design is built around the analytical work already completed by Xi Zixun and Wang Zhuoran:

interactive time-series charts for tracking visitor arrivals and China’s market share,
scatterplots and boxplots for period comparison and relationship checks,
clustering diagnostics such as elbow and silhouette views,
state maps and timelines that anchor cluster assignments back to historical months,
interactive data tables for cluster profiles and month-level assignments, and
model interpretation views for decision tree and random forest outputs.

The Shiny application will expose a focused set of controls, such as period filters, cluster parameters, random seeds, and export functions, so that users can test alternative settings without losing the interpretability of the workflow. The design principle is to keep each module analytically meaningful, visually legible, and easy to compare with the written coursework narrative.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
8. R Packages
The proposal relies on a set of packages that support data import, transformation, visualization, clustering, and modeling.







Package
Purpose




readxl
Import the cleaned Excel workbook and project sheets.

Filter, transform, summarize, and reshape monthly tourism data.

Parse dates and derive time-based fields such as year, month, and quarter.

Build the core statistical graphics used across all modules.

Add interactivity to selected EDA and CDA charts.

Combine multiple ggplot2 charts into shared analytical views.


GGally
Support paired visual exploration and relationship checks.


corrplot
Visualize correlation structures among tourism indicators.


cluster
Estimate k-means diagnostics and silhouette scores.


DT
Present interactive tables in the Quarto pages and Shiny application.


rpart
Train the decision tree classification model.


rpart.plot
Render readable static decision tree diagrams.


caret
Standardize model training, evaluation, and confusion-matrix reporting.


ranger
Fit the random forest classification model efficiently.


visNetwork
Support interactive tree-style model visualization.


htmlwidgets
Preserve interactive outputs inside the website.


shiny
Provide the modular interactive application framework for the final prototype.


bslib
Support theme control and layout customization in the Shiny interface.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics Prototype
text:
9. Expected Contribution of the Prototype
The proposed system contributes more than a set of charts. It offers a structured way to understand tourism recovery through three linked ideas:

recovery should be compared across historically meaningful periods;
the Chinese source market matters, but it does not fully explain overall tourism performance; and
monthly tourism conditions can be grouped into interpretable states that support both exploration and modeling.

By combining Xi Zixun’s descriptive and confirmatory work with Wang Zhuoran’s clustering module and the occupancy-classification module, the project moves from a general tourism dashboard toward a more analytical visual system for explaining recovery dynamics in Singapore.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics EDA
text: Singapore’s tourism sector experienced a severe shock during the pandemic and entered an uneven recovery phase in the years that followed. Monthly patterns in visitor arrivals, hotel occupancy, length of stay, and the share of the Chinese source market all suggest distinct recovery stages. Static charts alone are insufficient for users who need to explore these structural shifts from multiple perspectives. This project therefore proposes an interactive visual analytics prototype to help users understand how Singapore’s tourism market moved from pandemic shock toward recovery and high-performance states.

title: Singapore Tourism Recovery Visual Analytics EDA
text:
EDA

EDA 1 - Tourism Trends in Singapore

data_clean$date <- as.Date(data_clean$date)

eda1 <- ggplot(
  data_clean,
  aes(
    x = date,
    y = visitor_arrivals / 1e6,
    color = period,
    group = period
  )
) +
  geom_line(
    aes(
      text = paste0(
        "Date: ", format(date, "%Y-%m-%d"),
        "<br>Visitor Arrivals: ", round(visitor_arrivals / 1e6, 2), "M",
        "<br>Tourism Phase: ", period
      )
    ),
    linewidth = 1.1,
    alpha = 0.9
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "pre_covid" = "#2E8B57",
    "covid_shock" = "#E63946",
    "recovery" = "#457B9D"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "Singapore Visitor Arrivals Trend",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Visitor Arrivals (Millions)",
    color = "Tourism Phase"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(eda1, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(
    xaxis = list(
      title = "Date",
      tickformat = "%Y-%m"
    )
  )





Before the COVID-19 pandemic, the average number of visitors arriving in Singapore was around 1.5 million per month. However, due to lockdowns and international travel restrictions during the pandemic period, tourism dropped sharply to nearly zero. Since 2022, the tourism sector has gradually recovered. Nevertheless, possibly due to broader economic conditions, the number of visitors arriving in Singapore remains slightly lower than the pre-pandemic level.


EDA 2 - China Market Dynamics in Singapore Tourism Recovery

data_clean$date <- as.Date(data_clean$date)

p1 <- ggplot(data_clean, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(
      y = visitor_arrivals / 1e6,
      color = "Total Visitors",
      group = "Total Visitors",
      text = paste0(
        "Date: ", format(date, "%Y-%m-%d"),
        "<br>Total Visitors: ", round(visitor_arrivals / 1e6, 2), "M"
      )
    ),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  geom_line(
    aes(
      y = visitor_arrivals_china / 1e6,
      color = "China Visitors",
      group = "China Visitors",
      text = paste0(
        "Date: ", format(date, "%Y-%m-%d"),
        "<br>China Visitors: ", round(visitor_arrivals_china / 1e6, 2), "M"
      )
    ),
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(
    "Total Visitors" = "#457B9D",
    "China Visitors" = "#E63946"
  )) +
  labs(
    title = "China Market Contribution to Singapore Tourism Recovery",
    x = "Date",
    y = "Visitor Arrivals (Millions)",
    color = "Series"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

ggplotly(p1, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(
    xaxis = list(
      title = "Date",
      tickformat = "%Y-%m"
    )
  )




p2 <- ggplot(data_clean, aes(x = date)) +
  geom_line(
    aes(
      y = china_share_pct,
      group = 1,
      text = paste0(
        "Date: ", format(date, "%Y-%m-%d"),
        "<br>China Visitor Share: ", round(china_share_pct, 2), "%"
      )
    ),
    color = "#2E8B57",
    linewidth = 1
  ) +
  labs(
    title = "China Visitor Share Over Time",
    x = "Date",
    y = "China Visitor Share (%)"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()


ggplotly(p2, tooltip = "text") %>%
  layout(
    xaxis = list(
      title = "Date",
      tickformat = "%Y-%m"
    )
  )




eda2_combined <- p1 / p2

eda2_combined








This analysis compares total visitor arrivals with Chinese visitor arrivals and examines the share of Chinese visitors over time. Prior to the pandemic, Chinese visitors consistently accounted for a substantial portion of Singapore’s tourism demand and moved closely with overall visitor trends. During the COVID-19 shock in 2020–2021, both total and Chinese visitor arrivals declined sharply due to global travel restrictions. In the recovery period after 2022, total visitor arrivals rebounded more rapidly than Chinese visitor arrivals, indicating that Singapore’s tourism recovery was supported by multiple international markets rather than relying solely on the Chinese market.


EDA 3 - Tourism Demand and Hotel Utilisation

ggplot(
  data_clean,
  aes(x = visitor_arrivals/1e6, y = hotel_occ, color = period)
) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +

  geom_smooth(
    aes(group = 1),
    method = "lm",
    se = FALSE,
    color = "black",
    linewidth = 1.2
  ) +

  theme_minimal() +
  labs(
    title = "Relationship Between Tourism Demand and Hotel Utilisation",
    x = "Visitor Arrivals (Millions)",
    y = "Hotel Occupancy (%)",
    color = "Tourism Phase"
  ) 








This analysis examines the relationship between visitor arrivals and hotel occupancy in Singapore. The scatter plot shows a clear positive relationship, where higher visitor arrivals are generally associated with higher hotel occupancy rates. During the COVID-19 shock period, visitor arrivals dropped sharply to near zero due to travel restrictions, while hotel occupancy declined but remained above 40–60%. This suggests that hotel demand was partially supported by domestic staycations or essential travel. As tourism gradually recovered after 2022, both visitor arrivals and hotel occupancy increased, moving closer to pre-pandemic levels. Overall, the results indicate that hotel utilisation closely follows tourism demand.


EDA 4 - Tourism Demand and Visitor Spending Behaviour

eda4 <- ggplot(
  data_clean,
  aes(
    x = visitor_arrivals/1e6,
    y = spend_per_capita,
    color = period,
    text = paste0(
      "Date: ", format(date, "%Y-%m-%d"),
      "<br>Visitor Arrivals: ", round(visitor_arrivals/1e6,2), "M",
      "<br>Spend Per Capita: $", round(spend_per_capita,0)
    )
  )
) +

geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.8) +

geom_smooth(
  aes(group = 1),
  method = "lm",
  se = FALSE,
  color = "black",
  linewidth = 1.2
) +

scale_color_manual(values = c(
  "pre_covid" = "#2E8B57",
  "covid_shock" = "#E63946",
  "recovery" = "#457B9D"
)) +

labs(
  title = "Tourism Demand and Visitor Spending Behaviour",
  x = "Visitor Arrivals (Millions)",
  y = "Spend Per Capita (SGD)",
  color = "Tourism Phase"
) +

theme_minimal()

ggplotly(eda4, tooltip = "text")





This analysis explores the relationship between visitor arrivals and tourism spending behaviour. The results indicate that higher visitor arrivals are generally associated with higher tourism spending levels. However, the recovery period shows variations in spending behaviour, suggesting that tourism recovery may not only depend on visitor volume but also on visitor spending patterns. Understanding these patterns is important for evaluating whether tourism recovery reflects a return to high-value tourism or simply an increase in visitor numbers.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text: This review covers the clustering prototype draft in prototype/module-cluster.qmd, the supporting cleaned dataset, and the renderability of the updated Quarto page inside the full project.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text: This review covers the clustering prototype draft in prototype/module-cluster.qmd, the supporting cleaned dataset, and the renderability of the updated Quarto page inside the full project.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Findings In The Original Draft

Finding 1: The page still grouped too much code into single blocks
The earlier revision improved reproducibility, but several sections still bundled too many steps together. That made the page harder to read in presentation mode and less faithful to the “one code block, one result, one explanation” rhythm requested for this prototype.


Finding 2: Package loading and data import were not shown explicitly in the first draft
The earlier draft loaded and transformed data in a hidden setup chunk. That made the page less aligned with the Habari Tanzania reference style, where the reader can see the workflow directly.


Finding 3: The stay-duration feature was underspecified
The draft proposed avg_stay_monthly directly for clustering, but the cleaned data preparation already created avg_stay_monthly_capped for modeling. Using the uncapped series would let a small number of pandemic months dominate Euclidean distances.


Finding 4: The choice of k = 3 needed evidence
The draft said “start with K = 3”, but it did not show the elbow pattern, silhouette diagnostics, or the interpretability tradeoff behind the final selection.


Finding 5: The page was not yet submission-ready
The original file was a short module note, not a full prototype report. It lacked a visible end-to-end analytical workflow, detailed results sections, and a stronger explanation layer after each output.


Finding 6: Some narrative statements were too static
After the restructuring, a few explanatory sentences still assumed fixed outcomes, such as which state was the largest. That could drift out of sync if the dataset changed later.


Finding 7: The centroid table labels could be misread
The cluster-center table displayed standardized values, but the column names did not make that explicit enough. A reader could incorrectly read them as raw monthly averages.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Fixes Implemented

Rewrote prototype/module-cluster.qmd again into smaller analytical blocks.
Changed the structure so one focused code chunk now produces one immediate figure or table.
Added a short explanation directly after each output instead of collecting explanations too late.
Kept package loading and data import visible.
Retained avg_stay_monthly_capped as the modeling stay variable.
Preserved the code-generated elbow, silhouette, cluster profile, and timeline outputs.
Replaced static statements with result-driven narrative where appropriate.
Renamed the centroid columns so they clearly indicate standardized values.
Kept the narrative aligned with the Habari Tanzania reference structure while using k-means appropriately for continuous monthly data.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Residual Risks

The selected k = 3 solution is substantively stronger than the k = 2 solution, but the silhouette score is slightly lower. This tradeoff should still be acknowledged in the final coursework report.
china_share can look high during collapse months because it is a relative measure. The final narrative should always pair it with absolute arrivals.
If additional monthly indicators are added later, the cluster labels may need to be recalibrated.

title: Review Report: Clustering Prototype
text:
Review Outcome
No blocking issues remain in the updated clustering prototype. The page now matches the requested reproducible style much more closely: visible workflow, code-generated results, and explanation after each analytical step.

section: 1.5Retain the valid data suitable for monthly analysis
text:
1.5Retain the valid data suitable for monthly analysis


Show the code
monthly_clean <- raw_tbl %>%
  filter(
    !is.na(date),
    !is.na(avg_stay_monthly),
    !is.na(hotel_occ),
    !is.na(visitor_arrivals),
    !is.na(visitor_arrivals_china)
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    year = lubridate::year(date),
    month = lubridate::month(date),
    quarter = lubridate::quarter(date),
    period = case_when(
      date <= as.Date("2020-01-01") ~ "pre_covid",
      date <= as.Date("2021-12-01") ~ "covid_shock",
      TRUE ~ "recovery"
    ),
    china_share = visitor_arrivals_china / visitor_arrivals,
    china_share_pct = china_share * 100
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
1.6Handling extreme values


Show the code
stay_cap <- quantile(monthly_clean$avg_stay_monthly, probs = 0.95, na.rm = TRUE)

monthly_clean <- monthly_clean %>%
  mutate(
    avg_stay_monthly_capped = pmin(avg_stay_monthly, stay_cap),
    visitor_arrivals_millions = visitor_arrivals / 1000000,
    visitor_arrivals_china_thousands = visitor_arrivals_china / 1000
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
1.7Generate analysis fields


Show the code
occ_cuts <- quantile(monthly_clean$hotel_occ, probs = c(1/3, 2/3), na.rm = TRUE)

analysis_ready <- monthly_clean %>%
  mutate(
    cluster_z_visitor_arrivals = as.numeric(scale(visitor_arrivals)),
    cluster_z_china_share = as.numeric(scale(china_share)),
    cluster_z_hotel_occ = as.numeric(scale(hotel_occ)),
    cluster_z_avg_stay_monthly_capped = as.numeric(scale(avg_stay_monthly_capped)),
    hotel_occ_level_tertile = case_when(
      hotel_occ <= occ_cuts[[1]] ~ "low",
      hotel_occ <= occ_cuts[[2]] ~ "medium",
      TRUE ~ "high"
    ),
    hotel_occ_level_business = case_when(
      hotel_occ < 70 ~ "low",
      hotel_occ <= 85 ~ "medium",
      TRUE ~ "high"
    )
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    hotel_occ_level_tertile = factor(hotel_occ_level_tertile, levels = c("low", "medium", "high")),
    hotel_occ_level_business = factor(hotel_occ_level_business, levels = c("low", "medium", "high"))
  ) %>%
  mutate(
    dataset_split = if_else(row_number() <= floor(n() * 0.8), "train", "test")
  )

title: Visualization of classification trees and random forest analysis
text:
3.6OOB accuracy vs number of trees


Show the code
tree_grid <- seq(50, 300, by = 25)

oob_tbl <- purrr::map_dfr(tree_grid, function(nt) {
  mod <- ranger::ranger(
    hotel_occ_level_tertile ~ arrivals_million + china_share_pct + stay_days + month,
    data = train_df,
    num.trees = nt,
    mtry = rf_model$bestTune$mtry,
    min.node.size = rf_model$bestTune$min.node.size,
    splitrule = as.character(rf_model$bestTune$splitrule),
    importance = "impurity",
    seed = 1234
  )

  tibble(
    num_trees = nt,
    oob_error = mod$prediction.error,
    oob_accuracy = 1 - mod$prediction.error
  )
})

print(oob_tbl)
